content.heading: Event Listeners — responding to user actions

body.text: An event listener watches an element and runs a function when something happens — a click, a keypress, a mouse movement. You tell the browser what to watch and what to do when it happens.

body.desc: Watch this element — when it is clicked, run this function

lines: // The function does NOT run immediately
// It runs later, each time the button is clicked

body.label: Important
body.text: The function inside addEventListener does not run right away. It runs later, when the event fires. This is different from most code, which runs top to bottom immediately.

exercises.title: Event listener in isolation
tasks: Declare a variable called clickCount set to 0.
Add a click listener to the document: document.addEventListener('click', function() { ... })
Inside the listener: increment clickCount, then log 'Clicks: ' + clickCount.
Run the code, then click anywhere in the output area. Watch the console.
Challenge: also log the current time using new Date().toLocaleTimeString().
exercises.hint: document.addEventListener listens for clicks anywhere on the page — you do not need a specific button element.

exercises.title: Predict the listener
tasks: Read the starter code without running it. How many times will 'clicked!' be logged if you click the document 3 times?
Write your prediction, then run and click 3 times to check.
Now add a second addEventListener call to the same document for the same 'click' event.
Predict: does adding a second listener replace the first, or do both fire? Test it.
exercises.hint: Adding a second event listener does not replace the first — both run independently.
